coredata.dc:title: Facile approach for green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Manihot esculenta and their potential applications as sensor and bio-imaging agents
coredata.dc:creator: Sivanandhan, Monisha
Parasuraman, Amutha
Surya, Chinnasamy
Lakshminarayanan, Komalavalli
Krishnakumar, Balu
Mani, Durai
Ahn, Young-Ho
coredata.dc:description: An eco-friendly approach for the synthesis of blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using Manihot esculenta with an average size of 3–5 nm through hydrothermal method has been reported. Functionality of the prepared CDs was characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. Structural and morphological characterisation were carried with XRD and TEM techniques, these studies exposed amorphous nature of carbons dots with spherical shape. Optical properties of CDs were examined using UV–Visible and Photoluminescence spectral techniques. Owing to strong blue fluorescence, CDs were evaluated for its ability to sense various heavy metal ions. Attributable to significant response towards Fe3+ ions among different metal ions tested (Al3+, Cu+, Co2+, Pb2+ , Zn2+ , Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+ and Mg2+), CDs were used as a selective fluorescence probe for detection of Fe3+. The bioimaging and cytotoxicity aspects of CDs on MDA MB-231 breast cancer cell lines were confirmed by localised experiments. The synthesised CDs were found to be non-toxic even at their high concentration of 500 µg/mL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manihot esculenta
Hydrothermal technique
Fe3+ ion sensors
Cytotoxicity
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the Synthesis of CDs from hydrothermal method using Manihot esculenta.
FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots in a range of 400–4000 cm−1.
TEM image of CDs (a) scale bar is 10 nm and (b) scale bar is 50 nm, (c) SAED pattern of CDs.
Particle size distribution curve of CDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorption, (b) Photoluminescence behaviour of CDs, (c) Photo luminescent spectra of CDs measured at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm-400 nm, (d) image of CDs under UV irradiation.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of different metal ions in aqueous solution (b) Fluorescence spectral quenching of CDs upon addition of different concentration of Fe3+ ion ranging from 10 to 100 µM (c) The relationship of (Fo − F)/Fo versus the Fe3+ ion concentration.
(a and b) Cytotoxicity studies of CDs using MDA MB 231 cells.
(a) Bright light, (b) UV-330 nm, (c) Green-450 nm and (d) Red-510 nm fluorescence bio-imaging of MDA MB 231 breast cancer cells.
Comparison of limit of detection (LOD) and linear detection range for Fe3+ of carbon dots prepared from various green sources.

head.ce:title: Facile approach for green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Manihot esculenta and their potential applications as sensor and bio-imaging agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, PSGR Krishnammal College for Women, Coimbatore 641004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, PSGR Krishnammal College for Women, Coimbatore 641004, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Science and Engineering Laboratory, Department of Civil Engineering, Yeungnam University, Geongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Science and Engineering Laboratory, Department of Civil Engineering, Yeungnam University, Geongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Dhanalakshmi Srinivasan College of Arts and Science for Women (Autonomous), Perambalur 621 212, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dhanalakshmi Srinivasan College of Arts and Science for Women (Autonomous)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, Dhanalakshmi Srinivasan College of Arts and Science for Women (Autonomous), Perambalur 621 212, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the Synthesis of CDs from hydrothermal method using Manihot esculenta.

An eco-friendly approach for the synthesis of blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using Manihot esculenta with an average size of 3–5 nm through hydrothermal method has been reported. Functionality of the prepared CDs was characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. Structural and morphological characterisation were carried with XRD and TEM techniques, these studies exposed amorphous nature of carbons dots with spherical shape. Optical properties of CDs were examined using UV–Visible and Photoluminescence spectral techniques. Owing to strong blue fluorescence, CDs were evaluated for its ability to sense various heavy metal ions. Attributable to significant response towards Fe3+ ions among different metal ions tested (Al3+, Cu+, Co2+, Pb2+ , Zn2+ , Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+ and Mg2+), CDs were used as a selective fluorescence probe for detection of Fe3+. The bioimaging and cytotoxicity aspects of CDs on MDA MB-231 breast cancer cell lines were confirmed by localised experiments. The synthesised CDs were found to be non-toxic even at their high concentration of 500 µg/mL.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were primarily identified in 2004 during purification of Single-Walled Carbon nanotubes [1]. CDs are fairly spherical nanoparticles with size below 10 nm [2]. They earned massive attention due to their exclusive properties like high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, high chemical stability, biocompatibility, robustness, straight forward functionalization, etc. [3–6]. Carbon dots are better than classical quantum dots which have drawbacks like leaching of toxic metal ions [7,8] & molecular probes are liable to photo degradation as well [9]. Accordingly, fluorescent CDs are preferred as probes in biological applications due to low toxicity and stability. CDs were synthesized using different techniques viz., electrochemical deposition [10], ultrasonic treatment [11], laser ablation [12], combustion [13], microwave treatment [14], hydrothermal method [15] etc. Nevertheless, these methods involve strong acids, sophisticated experimental conditions, post treatment route and high cost [16,17]. Consequently, greener approaches are essential for synthesis of CDs without involving acidic chemicals and tedious procedure. Currently several researchers have been working on the preparation of fluorescent CDs by means of hydrothermal method with the help of renewable precursors. Hydrothermal approach is more beneficial due to simpler methodology, economic, avoids usage of organic reagents, faster and reaction could be controlled easily. Indeed, even waste biomasses can be promptly changed into fluorescent CDs by this technique. Briefly, various research groups developed CDs in a greener way from several natural sources viz., garlic [18], lemon peel [19], onion waste [20], orange juice [21], papaya [22], sweet potato [23], prawn shell [24], mushroom [25], banana juice [26], milk [27] etc., which finds applications in fields like biosensors, heavy metal ion detection, cell imaging and further more [16].

Fe3+ is one of the most important transition metal ions, significant in environmental and biological systems [28]. It is an essential element in human body which plays vital role as enzyme cofactor in catalysis, involved in cellular metabolism, oxygen carrier in myoglobin and haemoglobin [29]. Even excess amount of iron in human body will induce oxidation of proteins and lipids [30], which may cause cancer and will inhibit proper functioning of liver, heart and pancreas. The insufficiency of Fe3+ leads to anaemia, heart failure, diabetics, liver and kidney damage etc. [31,32]. Literature survey discloses different strategies for the identification of Fe3+ such as voltammetry [33], chromatographic techniques [34], Laser Ablation Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass spectrometry [35] and Atomic emission spectroscopy [36]. However, these methods are tedious, require long detection time and expensive instrumentation. Taking into account CDs have pulled attention in heavy metal ion sensing due to its high sensitivity, simple and cost-effective technique. Some of the research groups already investigated Fe3+ ions using CDs from various green materials like black soya beans [37], Bergamot [38], lychee waste [39], Prunus cerasifera fruits [40] etc. With motivation of these accomplishments, we synthesised fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal method from Manihot esculenta an edible root crop. Manihot esculenta serves as the third largest carbohydrates source after rice and white flour. It contains vitamins, proteins, fats, minerals and about 60% water. The as-prepared fluorescent CDs were used as a probe to sense Fe3+ selectively. Additionally, the CDs prepared from Manihot esculenta were evaluated for its biocompatibility, cytotoxicity and bioimaging studies using MDA MB-231 breast cancer cell lines as well.

Manihot esculenta were purchased from local market Coimbatore, India. Al(NO3)3, CuBr, Co(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, Fe2(SO4)3, CaCl2, Hg(NO3)2 and MgCl2 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Deionised water was used throughout the experiments.

The morphology and microstructure of CDs were examined using JEOL-JEM 2100 Transmission Electron Microscope (FE-TEM). Particle size were calculated from TEM image using ImageJ software. The functional groups present in CDs were confirmed using SHIMADZU Fourier Transformation Infra-Red spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Wide angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were acquired from third generation Empyrean, Malvern Panalytical, Netherland & X’PERT3 Powder diffractometer with Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.5416 Å) operating at 20 kV. The absorption spectra of CDs were recorded using JASCO UV–Visible spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Fluorescent spectra were recorded using SHIMADZU RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer with xenon lamp and excitation/emission slit width of 5 nm. Zeta potential of CDs are calculated using Zetasizer Nano ZS by Malvern Panalytical. Bio-imaging studies were performed using Nikon Eclipse’Ti microscope.

Manihot esculenta (250 g) washed, peeled off and cut into little pieces. It was then ground into fine paste and made up to 200 mL using deionised water. The solution was transferred to autoclave and heated to 160 °C for about 8 h. Later, the pulp was separated by simple filtration. The resulting brown solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 mins to remove insoluble particulates. Then the centrifugate was freeze dried to form the fluorescent CDs. The resulting product was dissolved in required amount of water (1 mg/mL) and kept in the refrigerator for future use. (Fig. 1 ).

To authenticate the selectivity of sensing, various metal ions (Al3+, Cu+, Co2+, Pb2+ , Zn2+ , Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+and Mg2+) were used. 100 µM solution of CDs and metal ions were prepared using deionized water. Solution of each metal ions (1.5 mL) was mixed with 3.5 mL of CDs (1 mg\mL) and left undisturbed for 20 mins at 25 °C. These mixtures were taken in a quartz cuvette and subjected to photoluminescence studies. Different concentration of metal ion (10–100 µM) which exhibited significant fluorescence quenching were prepared and analyzed further to examine its detection with CDs. The ratio of CDs and metal ions were maintained as above and the same procedure was followed to record the emission spectra. The fluorescence emission was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

To know whether CDs affects the liveliness of normal healthy cells or not, an in vitro cytotoxicity test was performed using MDA MB-231 Breast cancer cells. The cells were grown in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS. Approximately 10,000 cells in each well were incubated for 24 h in CO2 (5 %) incubator. After the incubation, cells were checked for their morphology. From each well, culture medium was replaced with fresh DMEM medium. Then CDs at different concentrations (0–100 & 100–500 µg/mL) was added to each cell and incubated for 18 h. In each well (3-[4,5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl]-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide) MTT (1 mg/mL) were added and incubation continued for about 4–6 h. After incubation, MTT were removed from each well followed by the addition of 100 % DMSO for dissolving the formazan crystals and absorbance was measured at 570 nm using photometer.

Human breast cancer MB MDA231 cells were cultured in T75 cm2 cell culture flask containing (DMEM) 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin under humid atmosphere of 95% air and 5% CO2. For bio imaging, cells were incubated with 500 mL of CDs for 30 mins at 37 °C then the cells were washed in warm PBS before imaging.

Typically, CDs were synthesised by hydrothermal method using Manihot esculenta as precursor at 160 °C for 8 h. The CDs showed blue fluorescence under UV light. These CDs were applied in sensing Fe3+ ion and imaging MDA MB-231 cancer cell lines.

The prepared CDs were characterised to examine their functionality and optical properties. The surface functional groups of carbon dots were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. In Fig. 2 broad band about 3274.87 cm−1 was ascribed to be OH stretching frequency and vibrations frequency at 2915.51 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching. Further stretching frequencies at 1678.36 cm−1 and 1587.42 cm−1 attributed to CO & CN stretching vibrations, respectively. Additional peak at 1386.82 cm−1 signifies CC stretching vibrations.

The optical properties of CDs were analyzed by UV–Visible and photo luminescent (PL) studies. UV–Visible spectrum (Fig. 7 a) shows a strong absorption band at 282 nm, analogous to π-π* transition [41]. The PL spectrum (Fig. 7b) displays a strong emission at 454 nm under excitation wavelength of 282 nm. Under illumination of UV–Visible light at 365 nm, aqueous solution of CDs exhibited bright blue luminescence (Fig. 7d). Similar to normal CDs the synthesised CDs also exhibited excitation dependent emission character. The PL excitation was carried out at different wavelength from 280 to 400 nm with increment of 10 nm. As shown in (Fig. 7c), the CDs displayed strongest emission at 455 nm under the excitation wavelength of 282 nm and with further increase in the wavelength, intensity decreases. This behaviour is result of recombination of electrons and holes produced at the surface of CDs [42,43]. The redshift at different excitation wavelength is probably due to different surface size of nano CDs but the proper explanation of this shift is still debatable.

Unique fluorescence property of carbon dots makes its remarkable in the field of sensors. CDs with hydrophilic functional groups show excellent selectivity to sense metal ions. Hydrothermally derived CDs from Manihot esculenta were investigated for its sensing ability towards different metal ions like Al3+, Cu+, Co2+, Pb2+ , Zn2+ , Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+and Mg2+ (Fig. 8 a). PL spectra were recorded for different metal ion solution containing definite amount of CDs. As shown in Fig. 8a, CDs show moderate decrease in fluorescence intensity for all the ions investigated except strongest fluorescent quenching was observed for Fe3+ ion. This results clearly reveal the selectivity of CDs towards Fe3+ ions and could be promoted as selective probe for sensing Fe3+ ion. This high selectivity is due to the transfer of photoelectron between surface groups of CDs with Fe3+ ion [44–46].

Further to analyse its sensitivity, diverse concentrations of Fe3+ ion has been prepared in the array of 10–100 µM with specific concentration of CDs and their corresponding fluorescent spectra has been recorded at an excitation wavelength of 282 nm. Fig. 8b shows a steady decrease in the intensity of fluorescence with increase in concentration of Fe3+ ion, exposing the sensitivity of the as prepared CDs towards Fe3+ ions. The calibration graph Fig. 8c showed good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9958) between Fe3+ concentration and difference of fluorescence intensity [(Fo − F)/Fo], where F and Fo is the fluorescence intensities of CDs in presence and absence of Fe3+ ions. The detection limit of the CDs was found to be 13.65 µM calculated using the formula LOD = 3σ/Slope. The detection limit of synthesized CDs was compared with various CDs obtained from plant origin (Table 1 ). Our work marks good and comparable detection limit for Fe3+ ion in correspondence to the work reported in the literature.

Cytotoxicity test plays a vital role for applying CDs as sensors in various biomedical applications. Hence it is crucial to examine the toxicity of prepared CDs to be used for potential application. Herein, to evaluate the toxicity MTT assay were performed in MDA MB-231 Breast cancer cell lines with concentrations from 0 to 100 & 100–500 µg/mL for 24 h. The cell viability results obtained from Fig. 9 a and b showed that the CDs were non-hazardous to breast cancer cell lines even at their high concentration up to 500 µg/mL and their IC-50 value was found to be 125 µg/mL, which suggests CDs as a viable candidate for biomedical / bioimaging applications of live cells.

CDs with extraordinary fluorescent properties and negligible cytotoxicity has been reported as an ideal probe for bioimaging studies [51–54]. Herein, MDA MB-231 breast cancer cells were used to analyse the efficiency of CDs as bioimaging agents. CDs along with MDA MB-231 breast cancer cells has been incubated for 4 h and then exposed to inverted microscope. These cells were brightly multi-coloured when exposed to various excitation wavelength 330 nm, 450 nm and 510 nm showed blue, red and green coloured fluorescent emission respectively (Fig. 10 ) These multicolour images suggest a choice of selecting wavelength for observation. These findings substantiate that the CDs are restrained into cell membrane and cytoplasm and restrict themselves from penetrating into the nucleus. This imprints that the prepared CDs act as a promising bioimaging agent without altering the liveliness of the cell.

In this work, we have deliberated an attractive approach of preparing highly potent CDs using Manihot esculenta in a greener pathway through hydrothermal method. The water soluble CDs obtained exhibit strong blue luminescence with good stability. CDs were proved to act as a good precursor for sensing heavy metal ions particularly Fe3+ ion. The prepared CDs exhibit good biocompatibility and can be utilized favourably as a fluorescence probe for bioimaging. Eventually, the less toxic and strong fluorescence CDs were employed effectively for bioimaging MDA MB 231 breast cancer cells, signifying their ability towads varied applications.

Monisha Sivanandhan is involved in the preparation of the CDs and specifically writing the initial draft. Amutha Parasuraman contributed in the supervision and leadership responsibility for the research activity planning and execution, including mentorship external to the core team. Chinnasamy Surya and Komalavalli Lakshminarayanan are involved in materials characterization and analysis. Balu Krishnakumar is involved in material characterization’s discussion, application and correcting the manuscript. Durai Mani and Young-Ho Ahn are involved in the validation of the manuscript and overall replication/reproducibility of results/experiments and other research outputs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank GRG Trust project for financial support. Financed by Ministry of Education, Basic Science Research Program-National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF-2020R1I1A3054816).

Chem. 84 (2012) 5351–5357. https://doi.org/10.1021/ac3007939.
